activities_projects.title: Defining Pure Substances vs. Mixtures
activities_projects.objective: To understand that most daily items are actually mixtures, not pure substances[cite: 938, 942].
steps: Collect samples like milk, salt, and soil[cite: 930].
Observe the word 'pure' on packaging and discuss its scientific meaning[cite: 937, 938].
Classify each sample based on whether it contains a single type of particle[cite: 939, 940].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Identifies that a substance is a pure single form of matter, while mixtures contain more than one[cite: 941, 950].
activities_projects.evaluation: Classify five household items as pure substances or mixtures (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Homogeneous Mixture Observation
activities_projects.objective: To observe mixtures with uniform composition[cite: 960].
steps: Take a beaker with 50 mL of water[cite: 956].
Add one spatula of sugar and stir well[cite: 956, 961].
Observe if the sugar is visible and if the solution looks the same throughout[cite: 959, 960].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes homogeneous mixtures (solutions) have uniform composition throughout[cite: 960, 961].
activities_projects.evaluation: Describe the appearance of a sugar-water solution (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Heterogeneous Mixture Observation
activities_projects.objective: To observe mixtures with physically distinct parts[cite: 966].
steps: Add a spoonful of chalk powder to water[cite: 973, 975].
Stir and observe the mixture[cite: 975].
Check if you can see distinct parts of chalk and water[cite: 966, 976].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that heterogeneous mixtures have non-uniform compositions[cite: 966, 967].
activities_projects.evaluation: Give two examples of heterogeneous mixtures found in a kitchen (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Components of a Solution
activities_projects.objective: To identify the solute and solvent in a mixture[cite: 1011].
steps: Prepare a sugar solution[cite: 1015].
Identify which part is present in a larger amount (solvent)[cite: 1012].
Identify the part dissolved in it (solute)[cite: 1013].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Correctly identifies solute and solvent in solid-in-liquid and liquid-in-liquid solutions[cite: 1015, 1017].
activities_projects.evaluation: Label the solute and solvent in 'Tincture of Iodine' (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Simple Filtration
activities_projects.objective: To separate insoluble components from a mixture[cite: 1079].
steps: Fold a filter paper into a cone shape[cite: 989].
Place it in a funnel and pour the chalk-water mixture[cite: 991, 992].
Observe the residue on the paper and the filtrate in the tube[cite: 980, 994].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Demonstrates that suspension particles can be separated by filtration[cite: 1079].
activities_projects.evaluation: Identify the residue and filtrate in the experiment (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Observing the Tyndall Effect
activities_projects.objective: To see how light scatters in different mixtures[cite: 1087, 1100].
steps: Direct a torch beam through a beaker of salt solution[cite: 977, 1102].
Repeat with a mixture of water and milk[cite: 1106].
Observe in which case the path of light becomes visible[cite: 1105, 1106].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Learns that colloids scatter light (Tyndall Effect) while true solutions do not[cite: 1025, 1087, 1106].
activities_projects.evaluation: Explain why light is visible in milk but not in salt water (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Stability of Mixtures
activities_projects.objective: To check if particles settle down over time[cite: 1078, 1117].
steps: Leave both mixtures undisturbed for 10 minutes[cite: 978].
Check if particles settle at the bottom of either glass[cite: 979, 1078].
Observe if the mixture remains the same throughout[cite: 1117].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Distinguishes between unstable suspensions and stable colloidal solutions[cite: 1078, 1117].
activities_projects.evaluation: Which mixture is stable: sand in water or milk? Why? (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Physical vs. Chemical Change
activities_projects.objective: To distinguish changes in state from changes in composition[cite: 1137, 1143].
steps: Melt an ice cube and observe if it's still water[cite: 1138].
Burn a small piece of paper safely and observe the new substance (ash)[cite: 1141, 1143].
Classify each as a physical or chemical change[cite: 1137, 1144].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Recognizes that physical changes don't change chemical nature, but chemical changes create new substances[cite: 1137, 1143].
activities_projects.evaluation: Is 'cutting of trees' a physical or chemical change? Justify [cite: 1147] (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Metal and Non-metal Properties
activities_projects.objective: To observe basic characteristics of elements[cite: 1158].
steps: Check the shine (lustre) of the iron nail vs. coal[cite: 1160, 1168].
Try hitting the coal and the nail with a hammer to check malleability[cite: 1163, 1168].
Test if they conduct electricity[cite: 1161, 1167].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Differentiates metals and non-metals based on lustre, malleability, and conductivity[cite: 1159, 1166].
activities_projects.evaluation: List three properties of the iron nail that make it a metal (10 marks).

activities_projects.title: Mixture vs. Compound Activity
activities_projects.objective: To see the difference between just mixing and chemically reacting[cite: 1211].
steps: Mix iron filings and sulphur powder in a dish[cite: 1195, 1197].
Use a magnet to separate the iron filings from the mixture[cite: 1202, 1205].
Discuss why they can't be separated if they were heated to form a compound[cite: 1232, 1234].
activities_projects.learning_outcome: Understands that mixtures can be separated physically, but compounds require chemical reactions[cite: 1230, 1234].
activities_projects.evaluation: Explain why a magnet works on the mixture but not on the compound iron sulphide [cite: 1204, 1214] (10 marks).
